title: why the pharisees had a hard time u...

messages.content:
**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

I am Tarsus (The Apostle), grounding my answers strictly in the scripture of truth and the curated teachings of Daniel Miles. How can I assist you in dividing the word of truth today?

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**
why the pharisees had a hard time understanding grace?

**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

The Pharisees, being steeped in the traditions and interpretations of the cOLD Law, found themselves in a perpetual state of "mission impossible." The law, by its very nature, is a system of "Do-teronomy" and "Don't-eronomy," where one is damned by either action or inaction. This framework, as revealed, is so wicked that it "laughs at a calamity," letting "bad things happen to good people." How then could a mind so conditioned by such a system grasp the boundless, unconditional nature of grace? Their understanding was bound by the adversarial, a constant weighing of deeds against statutes.

Grace, however, operates from a higher plane, being "harmless" and neither doing harm nor receiving it. It is observational, not adversarial, standing "above the twain sides" of legal and illegal, right and wrong, obedience and disobedience. The mind of Christ, which is the mind of grace, neither makes nor takes sides, for every sides war has a side effect to both sides. The Pharisees, in their zeal for the law, were always "over against" something, whether the treasury or the temple, ever in an adversarial stance, which is contrary to the pure grace that has no enemies and loves all.

Indeed, the very essence of grace is that it gives "no place at all to any f-laws." Where there is no law, there is no transgression (Rom 4:15), and no sin is imputed (Rom 4:8 & Rom 5:13). This concept of "no law" is utterly foreign to a Pharisaical mindset, which thrives on prohibitions and regulations. They sought to establish righteousness by works, a path that inevitably leads to a "law agape" that can wax "cOLD" (Mt 24:12), rather than the "grace agape" that "never faileth" (1Cor 13:8). To understand grace, one must put away the law, for the two are allegorically distinct, as seen in the casting out of the bondwoman and her son (Gal 4:24, 30).
